phaseSafeSequencing.note: Do not publish to Hacker News yet; do not create Reddit or Indie Hackers top-level posts in this slice.

drafts.label: LinkedIn Draft 1
drafts.audience: early-stage founders who have a product but are unsure what to say publicly
drafts.safeReason: It names the approval-first workflow and measurement gap without asking the audience to buy or pretending there is traction yet.
drafts.finalText:
We shipped the first version of Weekly Content Engine and learned the uncomfortable part fast: a good approval workflow still produces zero learning if nothing approved goes live.

So v2 is deliberately smaller: two owner-approved public drafts, tracked links, and a basic response summary.

The goal is not more content. It is knowing whether a founder/operator story, grounded in real notes and community pain, earns a click, reply, sample request, or waitlist join.

If the source material is weak, the system still says: no strong post today. Reputation beats volume.
drafts.intendedAudience: early-stage founder with a product but no repeatable distribution habit

contextTuple.persona: early-stage founder with a product but no repeatable distribution habit
contextTuple.community: LinkedIn founder/operator circle and X builder audience
contextTuple.phaseState: phase-1: two approved public posts, no HN launch, Reddit/Indie Hackers reply-only

editSafety.note: Owner may edit finalText before publishing, but must keep draftId and trackedDestination unchanged for attribution.

drafts.label: X Draft 2
drafts.audience: builders testing distribution with small public bets
drafts.safeReason: It is concise, source-grounded, and points to a measurable demo instead of pitching in a community thread.
drafts.finalText:
A content engine that never publishes is just a nicer notes app.

This iteration: 2 approved drafts, tracked links, and a cheap feedback artifact if posts=0.

No scheduler. No auto-DMs. Just: did anyone click, reply, request a sample, or join the waitlist?
